metadata.subject: Physical and Health Education
metadata.description: Physical and Health Education 10 transitions students toward greater independence in physical activity and health management. Students analyze their own movement strengths and weaknesses, design and implement personal fitness plans, and engage with a broader range of physical activities. Health content deepens to include CPR/AED training, advanced sexual health, critical analysis of health information, and strategies for personal and community advocacy. First Peoples perspectives on holistic wellness and community health remain central.
metadata.general_description: A course developing independent fitness planning, advanced health literacy, CPR/AED skills, and personal and community health advocacy — preparing students for senior secondary PHE and lifelong wellness.

big_ideas.statement: Understanding our strengths and weaknesses helps us to develop our personal fitness goals.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students at Grade 10 are ready to conduct honest self-assessments of their fitness and movement abilities and to use that self-knowledge as the foundation for purposeful, personally relevant fitness planning.

big_ideas.statement: Trying a variety of different physical activities increases our physical literacy.
big_ideas.elaboration: Breadth of physical experience — across individual, team, rhythmic, and outdoor activities — develops greater movement versatility and increases the likelihood that students will find activities they will pursue throughout their lives.

big_ideas.statement: Healthy choices, including physical activity, influence our physical, emotional, and mental well-being.
big_ideas.elaboration: At Grade 10, students develop a more integrated understanding of how lifestyle decisions — activity, nutrition, sleep, relationships, and substance choices — interact to shape overall wellness.

big_ideas.statement: Developing personal fitness plans can motivate us to be active for life.
big_ideas.elaboration: Learning to design, implement, monitor, and adapt a personal fitness plan builds both the practical skills and the intrinsic motivation needed for lifelong physical activity participation.

competencies.raw: Develop, refine, and apply fundamental movement skills in various physical activities; develop and apply movement concepts and strategies in different activities; apply methods for monitoring and adjusting exertion levels; demonstrate safety, fair play, and leadership in physical activities; identify preferred types of physical activity.
i_can_statements: I can apply and refine fundamental movement skills across a wide variety of physical activities, adapting to different contexts.
I can use movement concepts and tactical strategies to improve my performance and support teammates.
I can accurately monitor my exertion using heart rate, RPE, and other methods, and adjust my training intensity purposefully.
I can demonstrate consistent safety, fair play, and leadership in all physical activity settings.
I can articulate my physical activity preferences and connect them to a plan for lifelong active living.

competencies.raw: Participate daily in fitness-enhancing physical activity; describe how school, home, and community activities influence health; develop strategies for promoting healthy eating choices; assess factors influencing healthy choices; identify and analyze health message sources and their behavioural influence; identify and apply personal healthy-living goal strategies; reflect on outcomes and assess personal goal strategies.
i_can_statements: I can participate regularly in physical activity that builds and sustains my personal fitness.
I can analyze how lifestyle factors — activity levels, nutrition, sleep, and relationships — across school, home, and community influence my health.
I can develop and apply practical strategies for healthy eating in real-life contexts.
I can critically evaluate health information from media, social networks, and commercial sources for accuracy and bias.
I can design, implement, monitor, and revise a personal healthy-living goal plan, reflecting on outcomes with honesty.

competencies.raw: Propose strategies for avoiding and/or responding to unsafe or exploitative situations; propose strategies for responding to discrimination, stereotyping, and bullying; propose strategies for developing and maintaining healthy relationships; create strategies for promoting school and community well-being.
i_can_statements: I can develop and apply strategies for recognizing and responding to unsafe, exploitative, or coercive situations in person and online.
I can propose and model strategies for standing against discrimination, stereotyping, and bullying in my school and community.
I can create and apply strategies for developing healthy, respectful, and consensual relationships.
I can take initiative in promoting the health and well-being of my school and community.

competencies.raw: Describe and assess strategies for promoting mental well-being; describe and assess strategies for managing mental health and substance use problems; create and assess strategies for managing physical, emotional, and social changes during puberty; explore and describe transition and change impacts on identities.
i_can_statements: I can describe and evaluate a comprehensive range of strategies — including physical activity, social support, and self-care — for promoting mental well-being.
I can analyze mental health challenges and substance use issues, identifying effective strategies for support and intervention.
I can create personalized strategies for managing the ongoing physical, emotional, and social dimensions of adolescent development.
I can articulate how identity — including culture, gender, sexuality, and life experiences — shapes my well-being and future choices.

areas.name: Fundamental Movement Skills and Physical Activity
areas.raw: Fundamental movement skills applied across diverse activity contexts; physical activity types and strategies.
topics: Advanced movement skill application: transferring skills across activities and adapting to new contexts
Tactical decision-making: applying movement strategies in competitive and cooperative settings
Physical activity diversity: experiencing individual/dual sports, team games, outdoor/adventure activities, fitness activities, and dance
Personal physical activity preferences: identifying, exploring, and committing to activities for lifelong participation
Movement analysis: identifying personal movement strengths and areas for improvement
Leadership in physical activity: leading warm-ups, officiating, coaching peers, and promoting inclusion

areas.name: Fitness and Training
areas.raw: Personal fitness planning, FITT, SAID, fitness assessment, and training program design.
topics: Personal fitness assessment: testing and interpreting cardiovascular endurance, muscular strength/endurance, flexibility, and body composition
Fitness program design: applying FITT and SAID principles to create a balanced personal fitness plan
Progressive overload: understanding how to safely increase training demands over time
Recovery and injury prevention: rest, warm-up/cool-down, sleep, and hydration in training
Training log and monitoring: tracking progress, identifying patterns, and adjusting plans
SMART fitness goals: setting, pursuing, and evaluating personally meaningful fitness objectives

areas.name: Healthy Living and Decision Making
areas.raw: Nutrition, sexual health, substance use, CPR/AED, health advocacy, and emergency response.
topics: Advanced nutrition: sports nutrition, disordered eating awareness, and navigating food environments
Sexual health: consent, contraception, STI prevention and testing, and navigating sexual health services
Healthy and unhealthy relationships: recognizing coercion, abuse, and exploitation; accessing support
Psychoactive substances: cannabis legalization and youth, alcohol, tobacco, vaping — risks and decisions
CPR and AED: certified or instructional-level cardiopulmonary resuscitation and automated defibrillator use
First aid expansion: anaphylaxis response, concussion protocol, and emergency calling
Health advocacy: identifying local health resources and advocating for personal and peer health needs
Digital health literacy: evaluating online health information, apps, and social media influence

areas.name: Social and Community Health
areas.raw: Relationships, consent, community health, inclusion, discrimination, and First Peoples perspectives.
topics: Consent and communication: verbal and non-verbal consent, boundaries, and respectful communication
Healthy relationships: attributes, communication strategies, conflict resolution, and boundary-setting
Abusive relationships: warning signs, impact on health, and local resources for support
Gender and sexuality: exploring diverse identities with respect, accuracy, and inclusivity
Bystander intervention: skills for safely challenging discrimination, bullying, and harmful behaviour
Community health advocacy: peer education, anti-stigma campaigns, and school wellness initiatives
First Peoples concepts of relational and land-based wellness

areas.name: Mental Well-being
areas.raw: Mental health, stress, identity, help-seeking, resilience, and physical activity connections.
topics: Mental health literacy: understanding diagnoses, symptoms, and the mental health continuum
Stress management: evidence-based coping strategies, time management, and social support
Anxiety and depression: personal and community-level understanding, stigma reduction, and help-seeking
Substance use and mental health: the relationship between substance use and mental well-being
Identity and well-being: how culture, family, peer relationships, and experiences shape mental health
Resilience: developing personal strengths, coping flexibility, and recovery from setbacks
Physical activity as mental health strategy: evidence for exercise in mood regulation and stress reduction
